title: The Coming of Democracy and Coming to Terms with the Past
subject: History
caps_topic: The coming of democracy and coming to terms with the past

meta.title: The Coming of Democracy and Coming to Terms with the Past
meta.subject: History
meta.caps_topic: The coming of democracy and coming to terms with the past

sources.title: Negotiations toward a new South Africa - Grade 12
List of South African constitution negotiators
Nelson Mandela meets President P.W. Botha to begin negotiating an end to apartheid
Mandela suspends talks in the wake of the Boipatong massacre
Multi-party delegates endorse the interim constitution
President F.W. de Klerk announces South Africa's first democratic elections will be held
First democratic election results in South Africa

slides.title: The Coming of Democracy and Coming to Terms with the Past
slides.lead: How South Africa negotiated its way out of apartheid, held its first democratic election in 1994, and used the Truth and Reconciliation Commission to confront its past.

media.alt: A ballot box and a dove above a divided-then-joined landscape, symbolising a negotiated transition to democracy.

slides.notes: Open by framing the two linked halves of this CAPS topic. First, 'the coming of democracy': the negotiated settlement of 1990-1994 that dismantled apartheid without full-scale civil war. Second, 'coming to terms with the past': the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, South Africa's chosen mechanism of transitional justice. Ask learners to hold one analytical question across the lesson: was this transition a 'miracle', a negotiated compromise, or an unfinished revolution?

slides.title: Why negotiate? A stalemate by the late 1980s
slides.lead: Neither the apartheid state nor the liberation movements could win outright - which pushed both sides toward talks.

blocks.text: By the late 1980s apartheid was in deep crisis. Township revolt and repeated states of emergency showed the state could not restore order, while international **sanctions, disinvestment** and economic decline weakened the government. At the same time, the ANC could not overthrow the state by force. The result was a **mutually hurting stalemate**.

items: The end of the Cold War (from 1989) removed the government's claim that it was fighting a Soviet 'total onslaught'.
Secret talks between imprisoned Nelson Mandela and the government began under P.W. Botha in the late 1980s.
F.W. de Klerk, who became State President in 1989, judged that reform through negotiation was the only viable route.

slides.notes: Stress causation and multiple factors: internal resistance, economic pressure, sanctions, and the shifting global context after 1989 all combined. Introduce the concept of a 'mutually hurting stalemate' from conflict studies - a moment when both sides conclude that continued struggle costs more than compromise. Note that Mandela initiated exploratory talks from prison; negotiation did not begin with De Klerk alone. [SME] Confirm the preferred emphasis and dates for the Mandela-Botha meeting (5 July 1989).

slides.title: De Klerk unbans the movements; Mandela is freed
slides.lead: In one speech to parliament, F.W. de Klerk opened the door to a negotiated future.

blocks.text: On **2 February 1990** De Klerk unbanned the **ANC, PAC, SACP** and other organisations, lifted restrictions on the media and released many political prisoners. Nine days later, on **11 February 1990**, **Nelson Mandela** walked free after 27 years in prison.

items: Exiles could return and the liberation movements could organise openly and legally.
The Groote Schuur Minute (May 1990) and Pretoria Minute (August 1990) set ground rules; the ANC suspended the armed struggle.
Removing the ban was the essential precondition for any legitimate negotiations.

media.alt: An open prison gate with a raised fist emerging, symbolising Mandela's release and the unbanning of the movements.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='24' y='30' width='64' height='110' fill='#9aa77f'/><g stroke='#5c1414' stroke-width='5'><line x1='36' y1='34' x2='36' y2='140'/><line x1='52' y1='34' x2='52' y2='140'/><line x1='68' y1='34' x2='68' y2='140'/><line x1='84' y1='34' x2='84' y2='140'/></g><path d='M110 30 a70 70 0 0 1 0 110' fill='none' stroke='#c8b89a' stroke-width='4'/><g fill='#8a1f1f'><rect x='132' y='84' width='16' height='40' rx='4'/><rect x='128' y='66' width='6' height='24' rx='3'/><rect x='137' y='60' width='6' height='30' rx='3'/><rect x='146' y='64' width='6' height='26' rx='3'/><rect x='155' y='70' width='6' height='20' rx='3'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This slide marks the decisive turning point. Make clear the sequence: unbanning first (2 February), then Mandela's release (11 February). Explain the 'Minutes' as confidence-building agreements that made talks possible - the Groote Schuur and Pretoria Minutes dealt with the return of exiles, release of prisoners and suspension of the armed struggle. Ask learners why unbanning had to come before negotiation: a banned organisation cannot legitimately represent its people at the table.

slides.title: From negotiation to democracy, 1990-1998

blocks.alt: Timeline of the transition from 1990 to 1998.

events.label: CODESA I convenes at Kempton Park (Dec)
CODESA II deadlocks; Boipatong and Bisho massacres; Record of Understanding
Multiparty Negotiating Forum; Chris Hani assassinated; interim constitution agreed (Nov)
First democratic election (27 April); Mandela inaugurated (10 May)
TRC public hearings begin; final Constitution adopted
TRC hands over its final report (Oct)

slides.notes: Use this timeline as the spine of the lesson. Point out the pattern learners should analyse: negotiations were not smooth or linear. They repeatedly broke down (1992) and were rescued (Record of Understanding), which is why historians describe the transition as fragile and contingent, not inevitable. Return to this slide as each event is discussed.

slides.title: Violence and mistrust nearly derailed talks
slides.lead: The negotiations took place against a backdrop of political violence that both sides accused each other of fuelling.

blocks.text: Between 1990 and 1994 thousands died in political violence, especially clashes between ANC and **Inkatha Freedom Party (IFP)** supporters, allegedly stoked by a **'third force'** within the security establishment. Two massacres in 1992 brought talks to the brink.

items: Boipatong massacre (17 June 1992): about 45 residents were killed; the ANC suspended negotiations.
Bisho massacre (7 September 1992): Ciskei soldiers opened fire on ANC marchers, killing about 28 people.
Right-wing white groups and the homeland leaders threatened to wreck any settlement.

media.alt: A cracked negotiating table, symbolising talks breaking down under the strain of political violence.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='30' y='70' width='160' height='16' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='44' y='86' width='10' height='48' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='166' y='86' width='10' height='48' fill='#7a5a3a'/><path d='M108 70 L100 54 L116 54 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M110 70 L104 86 M110 70 L118 86' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4'/><path d='M108 54 L112 40 L104 40 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: This slide develops the analytical point that the transition could have failed. Explain the 'third force' allegation - evidence that elements of the security forces covertly fuelled violence to weaken the ANC. Boipatong triggered an ANC walkout and a period of 'rolling mass action'. Bisho exposed the danger of the homeland structures. Emphasise that leadership choices to return to the table, despite these provocations, were what saved the settlement. [SME] Casualty figures for Boipatong are usually given as 45; confirm the figure your department prefers.

slides.title: CODESA I and CODESA II
slides.lead: The Convention for a Democratic South Africa was the first multiparty attempt to negotiate a new constitutional order.

blocks.text: **CODESA I** convened on 20-21 December 1991 at the World Trade Centre in Kempton Park, where most parties signed a **Declaration of Intent** committing to an undivided, democratic South Africa. **CODESA II** (May 1992) then collapsed in **deadlock**.

items: Parties disagreed on the majorities needed to adopt a new constitution and on power-sharing.
The government wanted strong minority protections; the ANC wanted clear majority rule.
The IFP and some homeland leaders were uneasy about a strong central state.

media.alt: A round negotiating table ringed by seats of different colours, representing many parties meeting at CODESA.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><ellipse cx='100' cy='84' rx='60' ry='30' fill='#7a8b5a'/><ellipse cx='100' cy='80' rx='60' ry='30' fill='#9aa77f'/><g><circle cx='40' cy='80' r='9' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='70' cy='52' r='9' fill='#2f5233'/><circle cx='100' cy='46' r='9' fill='#e8a13a'/><circle cx='130' cy='52' r='9' fill='#3f6b45'/><circle cx='160' cy='80' r='9' fill='#5c1414'/><circle cx='130' cy='108' r='9' fill='#c8b89a'/><circle cx='70' cy='108' r='9' fill='#b7a98d'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Explain CODESA as multiparty (not just ANC-government): some 19 delegations took part, including homeland administrations and smaller parties. The Declaration of Intent was a genuine achievement of common ground. But CODESA II broke down over the technical-but-crucial question of the special majorities needed to write the constitution - a proxy for the deeper fight between majority rule and minority veto. Link the deadlock forward to why a different forum (the MPNF) was needed in 1993.

slides.title: The Record of Understanding and the MPNF
slides.lead: After the 1992 breakdown, bilateral trust-building and a new forum put the transition back on track.

blocks.text: On **26 September 1992** Mandela and De Klerk signed the **Record of Understanding**, a bilateral agreement on prisoners, hostels and the carrying of weapons that restored momentum. Talks resumed in 1993 through the **Multiparty Negotiating Forum (MPNF)**.

items: The Record of Understanding sidelined the IFP, which withdrew from talks in protest for a period.
Cyril Ramaphosa (ANC) and Roelf Meyer (government) became the key channel between the two main parties.
The assassination of SACP leader Chris Hani on 10 April 1993 raised the stakes and pushed parties to set an election date quickly.

slides.notes: Emphasise process: the shift from the failed multilateral CODESA to a combination of tight bilateral deals (Record of Understanding) plus a broader forum (MPNF). Highlight the Ramaphosa-Meyer 'channel' as an example of how personal trust between negotiators mattered. Analyse the Hani assassination as a paradox - a moment that could have ignited civil war but which Mandela's televised appeal for calm turned into pressure for a firm election date (announced soon after). [SME] Confirm the framing of the IFP walkout and its later return.

slides.title: The compromises that made democracy possible
slides.lead: Democracy was secured through negotiated guarantees that reassured the outgoing regime.

blocks.heading: Sunset clauses
blocks.text: Joe Slovo's 1992 proposal: a Government of National Unity and job security for civil servants for a fixed period, easing white fears of sudden change.

blocks.heading: Interim constitution
blocks.text: Endorsed on 18 November 1993, it set out 34 principles a final constitution had to meet and created the framework for the 1994 election.

blocks.heading: Government of National Unity
blocks.text: Parties with over 5% of the vote gained cabinet seats; those over 20% could name a deputy president - power-sharing for five years.

slides.notes: This is the analytical heart of the coming of democracy. Explain 'sunset clauses': SACP leader Joe Slovo argued the ANC should offer compromises - power-sharing and guarantees for existing civil servants and security forces - to remove the losing side's incentive to wreck the deal. Debate with learners: were these compromises a pragmatic price for a peaceful transfer of power, or did they entrench elements of the old order and slow transformation? [SME] Verify the GNU cabinet-seat threshold wording (parties above 5% for cabinet seats; above 20% for a deputy president).

slides.title: The first democratic election
slides.lead: For the first time, South Africans of all races voted in a single national election.

blocks.text: Voting took place over several days, with **27 April 1994** as the main polling day - now celebrated as **Freedom Day**. The IFP only agreed to take part days before the election, and its name was added to ballots by sticker. Turnout was huge, with long, patient queues across the country.

items: Roughly 19.7 million valid votes were cast in a peaceful poll.
It was a founding, non-racial election under universal adult suffrage.
The election delivered the legitimacy that decades of apartheid rule had lacked.

media.alt: A long queue of voters snaking toward a polling station on election day.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='120' width='240' height='40' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='176' y='58' width='48' height='62' fill='#9aa77f'/><rect x='190' y='74' width='20' height='46' fill='#5c1414'/><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='30' cy='96' r='8'/><rect x='24' y='106' width='12' height='24'/><circle cx='58' cy='94' r='8'/><rect x='52' y='104' width='12' height='26'/><circle cx='86' cy='96' r='8'/><rect x='80' y='106' width='12' height='24'/><circle cx='114' cy='94' r='8'/><rect x='108' y='104' width='12' height='26'/><circle cx='142' cy='96' r='8'/><rect x='136' y='106' width='12' height='24'/></g><path d='M18 132 q80 -8 150 -14' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6 5'/></svg>

slides.notes: Convey the human and symbolic power of 27 April: images of kilometres-long queues, elderly people voting for the first time. Explain the last-minute IFP participation, which averted a boycott and possible violence in KwaZulu-Natal. Discuss legitimacy as a key concept - apartheid governments were never chosen by most South Africans, so this election was 'founding' in that it created a state the majority recognised as its own. [SME] The commonly cited valid-vote figure is about 19.7 million; confirm against the department's preferred source.

slides.title: The result and the Government of National Unity
slides.lead: The ANC won a clear majority, and a power-sharing government took office under Nelson Mandela.

blocks.text: The **ANC won about 62.6%** of the vote, the **National Party about 20.4%**, and the **IFP about 10.5%**. As agreed, a **Government of National Unity** was formed. Nelson Mandela was inaugurated as President on **10 May 1994**, with Thabo Mbeki and F.W. de Klerk as deputy presidents.

items: The ANC won a majority but not the two-thirds needed to write the final constitution alone.
The NP and IFP both took seats in the cabinet under the power-sharing deal.
The final Constitution, honouring the agreed principles, was adopted in 1996.

slides.notes: Give learners the headline results but focus analysis on their consequences. The ANC's majority fell short of two-thirds, which meant the final constitution still had to be negotiated in the Constitutional Assembly - reinforcing consensus politics. Note that De Klerk's NP later withdrew from the GNU in 1996, showing the limits of enforced power-sharing. Link the 1996 Constitution back to the 34 principles agreed in the interim constitution. [SME] Vote percentages (ANC 62.65%, NP 20.39%, IFP 10.54%) are widely reported; verify exact figures.

slides.eyebrow: Coming to terms with the past - 1995
slides.title: Establishing the Truth and Reconciliation Commission
slides.lead: Rather than trials like Nuremberg or a blanket amnesty, South Africa chose a public reckoning with its past.

blocks.text: There is no future without forgiveness.
blocks.cite: Archbishop Desmond Tutu, Chairperson of the TRC

blocks.text: The TRC was created by the **Promotion of National Unity and Reconciliation Act (Act 34 of 1995)** and chaired by **Archbishop Desmond Tutu**. Its purpose was to establish as complete a picture as possible of gross human rights violations committed between 1960 and 1994, and to promote national unity and reconciliation.

items: It offered a 'third way' between prosecuting everyone (retributive justice) and forgetting (blanket amnesty).
Its approach drew on ideas of restorative justice and the concept of ubuntu.
Victims were given a public platform to tell their stories and be officially acknowledged.

slides.notes: Frame the TRC as transitional justice - the choices a society makes about accountability when moving from authoritarian rule to democracy. Compare briefly with alternatives: Nuremberg-style trials (risked destabilising the settlement and the security forces) versus a general amnesty and silence (denied victims justice and truth). Explain restorative justice and ubuntu as the philosophical basis Tutu emphasised. Note the mandate covered violations by all sides, including the liberation movements, not only the state.

slides.title: Three committees, one process
slides.lead: The Commission gathered truth through public testimony and a conditional amnesty.

blocks.heading: Human Rights Violations
blocks.text: Heard the testimony of victims and survivors in emotional public hearings broadcast to the nation from 1996.

blocks.heading: Amnesty
blocks.text: Could grant amnesty to perpetrators who made full disclosure of politically motivated crimes - truth in exchange for freedom from prosecution.

blocks.heading: Reparation and Rehabilitation
blocks.text: Recommended how victims should be supported and compensated by the state.

media.alt: A microphone before an audience, representing public testimony at the TRC hearings.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='94' y='58' width='12' height='46' rx='6' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='96' y='104' width='8' height='24' fill='#5c1414'/><rect x='84' y='128' width='32' height='6' rx='3' fill='#5c1414'/><path d='M84 78 a16 16 0 0 0 32 0' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3'/><g fill='#c8b89a'><circle cx='40' cy='118' r='7'/><circle cx='62' cy='120' r='7'/><circle cx='138' cy='120' r='7'/><circle cx='160' cy='118' r='7'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the crucial and controversial trade at the centre of the TRC: conditional amnesty. Amnesty was not automatic - applicants had to fully disclose the truth about acts that were proportionate and politically motivated. Contrast the deeply moving victim hearings (Human Rights Violations Committee) with the more legalistic amnesty hearings. Ask learners: is exchanging amnesty for truth a fair price? What does a victim gain, and what do they give up?

slides.title: The TRC: achievements and criticisms
slides.lead: The TRC is admired worldwide, but historians and South Africans still debate how much it achieved.

blocks.text: The Commission heard over 20,000 statements and handed over its main **final report in October 1998** (with further volumes later). It documented atrocities that had been denied, gave victims public recognition, and helped prevent a cycle of revenge. Yet its record is contested.

items: Achievements: it exposed hidden truths, humanised victims, and offered a model of peaceful reckoning studied around the world.
Criticisms: amnesty denied some families justice; reparations to victims were widely seen as slow and inadequate.
Many key perpetrators refused to co-operate, and some questioned whether 'reconciliation' was really achieved.

slides.notes: Guide learners to a balanced, evidence-based judgement rather than a simple verdict. Achievements: acknowledgement, record-building, and the avoidance of retributive bloodshed. Criticisms: victims often felt short-changed when perpetrators walked free after confessing, and when promised reparations were slow. Some argue the TRC secured 'truth' more than 'reconciliation', and that it did not address the structural economic legacy of apartheid. Encourage learners to weigh both sides. [SME] Confirm the statement/hearing figures and the exact date of the final report handover (October 1998; additional volumes 2003).

slides.title: A negotiated democracy and an unfinished reckoning
slides.lead: South Africa avoided civil war through compromise, then tried to heal through truth-telling - both achievements remain debated.

blocks.text: The transition of 1990-1994 shows history made by human choices under pressure: a stalemate, a breakthrough, repeated breakdowns, and hard-won compromises that produced a democratic election and a power-sharing government. The **TRC** then tried to confront the past through truth rather than revenge - a bold experiment whose successes and shortcomings are still argued over today.

blocks.question: Explain why the negotiations between 1990 and 1994 can be described as fragile and easily derailed. Refer to at least two specific events.
How useful were the 'sunset clauses' and the Government of National Unity in securing a peaceful transition to democracy?
'The TRC achieved truth but not full reconciliation.' Do you agree? Support your view with evidence.

slides.notes: Draw the two halves of the topic together: the coming of democracy (a negotiated, contingent political settlement) and coming to terms with the past (transitional justice through the TRC). Use the three questions to assess source-analysis and extended-writing skills at Grade 12 level; the mark allocations mirror CAPS essay and paragraph conventions. Remind learners that both the negotiated compromises and the TRC's unfinished business connect directly to debates in present-day South Africa.
